5-1.v: Internal controls are processes and procedures designed to provide REASONABLE ASSURANCE that an organization achieves its objectives in operations, financial reporting, and compliance. They protect assets, ensure accurate records, and promote operational efficiency.
The Committee of Sponsoring Organizations (COSO) framework is the gold standard for internal control. It has five integrated components:

v: 1. Control Environment: The foundation — tone at the top, ethical values, board oversight, organizational structure, competence commitment
2. Risk Assessment: Identifying and analyzing risks that threaten achieving objectives; includes fraud risk assessment
3. Control Activities: Policies and procedures that mitigate risks — authorizations, reconciliations, physical controls, segregation of duties
4. Information & Communication: Relevant information must be identified, captured, and communicated in a timely manner
5. Monitoring Activities: Ongoing evaluations and separate evaluations to ensure controls are present and functioning

5-1.v: Each of the five components contains specific PRINCIPLES that support it. All 17 principles must be present and functioning for internal control to be effective. The IMA exam tests these principles directly.

r: Commitment to integrity and ethical values
Board exercises oversight responsibility (independence from management)
Management establishes structure, authority, and responsibility
Commitment to attract, develop, and retain competent individuals
Individuals are held accountable for their internal control responsibilities
Specify suitable objectives (clear enough to identify related risks)
Identify and analyze risks to achieving objectives
Identify and assess changes that could significantly impact internal control
Select and develop control activities that mitigate risks
Select and develop general IT controls over technology
Deploy control activities through policies and procedures
Use relevant quality information to support internal control
Communicate with external parties regarding internal control matters
Conduct ongoing and/or separate evaluations of internal control
Evaluate and communicate deficiencies to responsible parties

5-1.v: Exam Tips — 17 Principles: Control Environment = Principles 1–5 (ethics, board, structure, competence, accountability). Risk Assessment = 6–9 (objectives, risk id, fraud risk, change). Control Activities = 10–12 (controls, IT controls, policies). Information & Communication = 13–15 (quality info, internal, external). Monitoring = 16–17 (ongoing/separate evaluations, communicate deficiencies). All 17 must be present and functioning.

r: Alternative controls when primary isn't feasible
Manager review when segregation isn't possible

5-1.v: The most fundamental control principle. No single person should have control over all phases of a transaction. The four key duties that must be SEPARATED:

v: Authorization: Approving transactions
Recording: Entering transactions in the accounting system
Custody: Physical possession of assets
Reconciliation: Comparing records to physical assets
Internal controls provide only REASONABLE (not absolute) assurance
Collusion: Two or more employees working together can override controls
Management Override: Management can bypass controls — a key fraud risk
Cost-Benefit: Controls should not cost more than the benefit they provide
Human Error: Mistakes happen even with good controls in place
Pressure/Incentive: Financial need, performance targets, personal problems
Opportunity: Weak internal controls, ability to override controls
Rationalization: Justifying the fraud ('I deserve this', 'I'll pay it back')

5-1.v: Exam Tips: COSO has 5 components and 17 principles — Control Environment is the FOUNDATION (Principles 1–5). Segregation of Duties = most important preventive control. Preventive > Detective (better to prevent than detect). Reasonable assurance, NOT absolute assurance. Collusion and management override can bypass most controls. Fraud Triangle: Pressure + Opportunity + Rationalization. All 17 principles must be present and functioning for ICFR to be effective — the absence of any one principle is a deficiency.

5-2.v: Risk assessment is the second component of the COSO framework. It involves identifying and analyzing RISKS that could prevent the organization from achieving its objectives — including financial reporting, operational, and compliance objectives.

r: Risk level BEFORE any controls are applied
Risk level AFTER controls are applied
Cash risk after reconciliations and dual controls
Risk that controls fail to prevent/detect errors
Risk that audit procedures miss a material error

5-2.v: Enterprise Risk Management (ERM) — COSO ERM Framework
ERM is a broader framework that goes beyond financial controls to manage ALL risks that could affect the organization's strategy and value creation. COSO ERM (2017) has five components:

v: 1. Governance & Culture: Leadership sets risk appetite and culture
2. Strategy & Objective-Setting: Aligning risk appetite with strategy
3. Performance: Identifying, assessing, and prioritizing risks
4. Review & Revision: Monitoring risk management practices
5. Information, Communication & Reporting: Sharing risk information

r: The AMOUNT and TYPE of risk an organization is willing to accept in pursuit of its objectives — set by the board
The ACCEPTABLE VARIATION around the risk appetite — the operational range within which management works
The level at which risk becomes unacceptable and triggers escalation or action

v: AVOID: Eliminate the risk by not engaging in the activity
REDUCE: Implement controls to lower likelihood or impact
SHARE/TRANSFER: Insurance, outsourcing, hedging
ACCEPT: Acknowledge risk and do nothing (when cost > benefit)

5-2.v: Exam Tips: Inherent risk = BEFORE controls. Residual risk = AFTER controls. ERM is broader than COSO IC — covers strategy and value. Risk appetite is set by the BOARD. Four risk responses: Avoid, Reduce, Share, Accept. Risk tolerance = acceptable variation around appetite. Higher inherent risk → need stronger controls to reduce residual risk.

5-3.v: Control Activities — The Third COSO Component

Control activities are the ACTIONS (policies and procedures) that help ensure management directives are carried out and that risks are mitigated to an acceptable level. They are the most visible and operational component of internal control.

r: Requiring manager approval for expenses above $5,000; dual signatures on large checks
Monthly bank reconciliations; comparing physical inventory to records
Locked safes, security cameras, badge access, fencing
Budget-to-actual variance analysis; comparing results over time
Edit checks, data validation, system access controls
Dual approvals, access controls, SOD, data input validation
Bank reconciliations, audits, exception reports, variance analysis
Adjusting entries, system patches, disciplinary action

v: Hard Controls (Formal): Written policies, system access restrictions, approval workflows — easily observable and testable
Soft Controls (Informal): Ethical culture, management integrity, employee competence, tone at top — harder to observe but equally important

5-3.v: Key Control Activities for Common Business Processes

v: Cash Receipts: Daily deposits, restrictive endorsements on checks, independent reconciliation
Purchasing/AP: Purchase orders, three-way match (PO, receiving report, invoice), separation of purchasing from payment
Payroll: HR approval for hiring/pay rates, separate payroll preparation and distribution, regular review of payroll register
Fixed Assets: Capitalization policy, periodic physical inventory, authorized approval for acquisitions/disposals

5-3.v: Exam Tips: Control activities = the ACTIONS (policies/procedures) that mitigate risks. Preventive is BETTER than detective. Three-way match = PO + Receiving Report + Invoice. SOD is the MOST important preventive control. Physical controls protect ASSETS. Reconciliations are DETECTIVE. Authorization = who can approve. Remember: hard controls are formal/documented; soft controls relate to culture.

5-4.v: Modern businesses rely on information systems for virtually all financial transactions. Weak IT controls can undermine ALL other internal controls. The CMA exam tests both general IT controls (GITC) and application controls.
GITC apply to the overall IT environment and provide the foundation for all application controls:

r: Who can access systems, data, and programs
User IDs, passwords, multi-factor authentication, role-based access
Controlling changes to programs and systems
Backup procedures, job scheduling, incident response

5-4.v: Application controls are SPECIFIC to individual systems/applications and ensure completeness, accuracy, and validity of transactions:

v: Input Controls: Data validation, format checks, range checks, completeness checks — ensure accurate data entry
Processing Controls: Run-to-run totals, batch controls — ensure data is processed completely and accurately
Output Controls: Review of output reports, distribution controls — ensure output is complete and goes to right people
Systems Analyst: Designs system specifications
Programmer: Writes and modifies code
Computer Operator: Runs the system day-to-day
Data Entry: Inputs transaction data
These functions must be SEPARATED — no one should both program AND operate a system
Data residency: Where is data physically stored?
Shared responsibility model: Cloud provider secures infrastructure; client secures data/applications
Key risks: Vendor dependency, data breach, compliance with regulations
SOC Reports (SOC 1, SOC 2): Third-party assurance reports on service organization controls

r: Data accessible only to AUTHORIZED users
Data is ACCURATE and complete — not tampered with
Hash functions, digital signatures, audit trails

5-4.v: COBIT (Control Objectives for Information and Related Technologies) is the leading IT GOVERNANCE framework, developed by ISACA. While COSO provides the internal control framework for the whole organization, COBIT provides the governance and management framework specifically for ENTERPRISE IT. The two frameworks are complementary — COBIT maps to and extends COSO for the IT environment.

r: Board-level governance — set IT direction, evaluate IT investments, monitor IT performance
Strategic IT planning — align IT with business strategy, manage resources
Implement IT solutions — acquire and deploy technology and changes
Deliver IT services — ensure security, service continuity, and support
Monitor IT performance and controls — assess compliance

v: COBIT applies IT governance principles: stakeholder needs, covering the enterprise end-to-end, applying a single integrated framework, enabling a holistic approach, separating governance from management
COBIT vs COSO: COSO = broad internal control framework for the entire organization; COBIT = IT-specific governance and control framework. COBIT maps its control objectives to COSO components
COBIT vs ITIL: COBIT = governance and control (WHAT to do); ITIL = IT service management best practices (HOW to deliver services)
Key concept: COBIT helps management ensure IT aligns with business objectives, resources are used responsibly, and IT risks are managed appropriately

5-4.v: Exam Tips: GITC = GENERAL controls (apply to all systems). Application controls = SPECIFIC to one application. Input → Processing → Output. CIA Triad: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability. In IT SOD: Analyst, Programmer, Operator, Data Entry must be separated. SOC 2 = service organization security and availability. Encryption protects CONFIDENTIALITY. Audit trails protect INTEGRITY. COBIT = IT GOVERNANCE framework (ISACA) — complementary to COSO; covers Evaluate/Direct/Monitor, Align/Plan/Organize, Build/Acquire/Implement, Deliver/Service/Support, Monitor/Evaluate/Assess. COBIT = governance (WHAT); ITIL = IT service delivery (HOW).

5-5.v: The Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX) was enacted in 2002 following major corporate scandals (Enron, WorldCom, Tyco). It was designed to restore investor confidence by strengthening corporate governance, financial reporting integrity, and accountability of management.

r: CEO and CFO must PERSONALLY CERTIFY accuracy of financial reports
Management must ASSESS and REPORT on internal controls over financial reporting (ICFR); external auditor must attest
Companies must DISCLOSE material changes on a RAPID BASIS (real-time disclosure)
Criminal penalties for knowingly certifying false reports — up to $5M fine, 20 years prison

5-5.v: Section 404 — Internal Control over Financial Reporting (ICFR)

v: Management must establish, maintain, and ASSESS the effectiveness of ICFR
External auditors of large accelerated filers must ATTEST to management's assessment
Framework used: COSO — the standard for evaluating ICFR
Annual report must include management's conclusion on whether ICFR is EFFECTIVE
SOX requires ALL audit committee members to be INDEPENDENT directors
At least one member must be a 'financial expert'
Audit committee oversees the external auditor (not management)
Audit committee is the primary point of contact for internal and external auditors
Whistleblower programs: audit committee must establish procedures for anonymous reporting

External auditors CANNOT provide certain non-audit services to audit clients: bookkeeping, financial IS design, appraisal, actuarial services, internal audit outsourcing, management/HR functions, legal services
Lead audit partner must ROTATE every 5 years
Audit firm cannot audit a company if the CEO/CFO worked for that firm in the past year

5-5.v: Exam Tips: SOX Section 302 = CEO/CFO certify. Section 404 = management assesses ICFR, auditor attests. Section 409 = rapid disclosure. Section 906 = criminal penalties. Audit committee = ALL independent, at least one financial expert. External auditors CANNOT do internal audit outsourcing. Lead partner rotates every 5 YEARS. SOX applies to PUBLIC companies (SEC registrants).

5-6.v: Internal auditing is an INDEPENDENT, OBJECTIVE assurance and consulting activity designed to add value and improve an organization's operations. It helps organizations achieve objectives by evaluating risk management, control, and governance processes.

The Institute of Internal Auditors (IIA) defines internal auditing as providing assurance and consulting — evaluating the adequacy and effectiveness of governance, risk management, and control processes.

v: Assurance Engagements: Objective assessment of evidence — financial audits, operational audits, compliance audits, IT audits
Consulting Engagements: Advisory services — process improvement recommendations, control design advice
Fraud Investigations: Respond to fraud allegations and quantify losses
Compliance Audits: Verify adherence to laws, regulations, and policies
The internal audit CHARTER defines the purpose, authority, and responsibility of the internal audit function
Chief Audit Executive (CAE) must report FUNCTIONALLY to the Audit Committee (not management) for true independence
Internal auditors must be INDEPENDENT of the activities they audit — no operational responsibilities
Dual reporting: administratively to CEO, functionally to Audit Committee

5-6.v: Modern internal audit uses a RISK-BASED approach: focus audit resources on the HIGHEST RISK areas where controls may be weakest and consequences of failure are greatest.

Exam Tips: Internal audit reports to AUDIT COMMITTEE (functionally) — not to management. External audit reports to SHAREHOLDERS. IIA = standards for internal audit. CAE = Chief Audit Executive. Assurance ≠ Consulting (both valid IA activities). Independence = no operational responsibilities. Risk-based audit = focus on HIGH RISK areas. Internal audit adds VALUE — not just checks compliance.

r: THEFT of company assets — most common (89% of cases)
Cash skimming, larceny, payroll fraud, expense reimbursement fraud, inventory theft
Intentional MISSTATEMENT of financial statements — least common but highest dollar impact
Overstate revenue, understate expenses, hide liabilities, manipulate reserves
Misuse of position for personal gain at expense of employer
Bribery, kickbacks, conflicts of interest, bid rigging

v: PRESSURE (Incentive): Financial need, lifestyle pressures, performance targets, bonuses tied to results
OPPORTUNITY: Weak controls, ability to override controls, access to assets without oversight
RATIONALIZATION: Justifying theft — 'I deserve it,' 'I'll pay it back,' 'Everyone does it'
Note: The Fraud DIAMOND adds a 4th element — CAPABILITY (skills, authority, knowledge to commit and conceal fraud)
Behavioral: Lavish lifestyle beyond known salary, reluctance to take vacation, excessive overtime (to cover tracks), unusual emotional stress
Financial: Unexplained variances, missing documents, altered documents, duplicate payments
Control: Override of controls, bypassing normal procedures, excessive journal entries, round-number transactions

r: Benford's Law analysis, duplicate payment detection, outlier identification
Anonymous reporting — most frauds discovered this way (40%+ per ACFE)
Regular counts to detect asset theft

5-7.v: In naturally occurring datasets, the FIRST DIGIT follows a predictable pattern: '1' appears ~30% of the time, '2' ~18%, and higher digits less frequently. Fraudsters often use round numbers or numbers just below approval thresholds — these show unusual digit frequencies that Benford's Law can detect.

Exam Tips: Asset misappropriation = MOST COMMON fraud (89%). Financial statement fraud = MOST COSTLY per case. Fraud Triangle: Pressure + Opportunity + Rationalization. Fraud Diamond adds CAPABILITY. Whistleblower hotlines detect the MOST frauds. Benford's Law: first digit '1' = ~30%. Skimming = theft BEFORE recording. Larceny = theft AFTER recording. Surprise audits are very effective deterrents.

5-8.v: Corporate governance is the SYSTEM of rules, practices, and processes by which a company is directed and controlled. It balances the interests of stakeholders including shareholders, management, customers, suppliers, financiers, government, and the community.

v: Elected by SHAREHOLDERS — represents their interests
Primary duties: Hire/fire CEO, set strategy, oversee risk management, approve major decisions
FIDUCIARY DUTY to act in shareholders' best interests
Independent directors: No current/recent financial relationship with company — required for audit, compensation, and nominating committees

r: ALL independent directors; at least 1 financial expert
Oversees financial reporting, internal controls, external audit, internal audit, SOX compliance
Sets executive pay — prevents self-dealing by management

5-8.v: The AGENCY PROBLEM arises when AGENTS (management) make decisions on behalf of PRINCIPALS (shareholders), but their interests diverge. Management may maximize their own compensation/job security at shareholders' expense.

v: Solutions: Align incentives (stock options, performance pay), board oversight, transparent disclosure, executive compensation tied to long-term performance
Moral hazard: Agents take excessive risks knowing others bear the consequences
Adverse selection: Difficulty selecting honest and competent agents upfront
Say-on-pay: Shareholders vote (advisory) on executive compensation packages
Clawback provisions: Recover compensation paid based on restated financials
Stock ownership requirements: Executives must hold significant equity to align interests
Dodd-Frank Act (2010): Enhanced disclosure of CEO pay ratio, clawback rules, say-on-pay

r: Balance interests of ALL stakeholders (employees, customers, community, environment)

5-8.v: Exam Tips: Board = elected by SHAREHOLDERS. Agency problem = management (agent) vs shareholders (principal). Audit committee = ALL independent + 1 financial expert. Say-on-pay = ADVISORY vote (not binding). Clawback = recover compensation after restatement. Fiduciary duty = loyalty + care. Independent directors: no financial relationship with company. Stakeholder theory > shareholder theory for long-term sustainability.

5-9.v: Ethics refers to moral principles governing behavior. Strong organizational ethics is a KEY component of the COSO Control Environment — without it, all other controls are undermined. The IMA (Institute of Management Accountants) governs management accountants' conduct through its Statement of Ethical Professional Practice (also informally known as the IMA Code of Ethics).
IMA Statement of Ethical Professional Practice — Four Principles

r: Being truthful, candid, and free from deception
Reporting accurate results even when unfavorable
Being objective and avoiding conflicts of interest
Disclosing relationships that could affect independence
Not skewing analysis to support a predetermined conclusion
Meeting deadlines, maintaining competence, reporting wrongdoing

v: Competence: Maintain professional knowledge, perform duties per applicable laws and standards, provide accurate information
Confidentiality: Keep information confidential except when authorized or legally obligated to disclose
Integrity: Avoid conflicts of interest, refuse gifts that would influence behavior, communicate unfavorable news
Credibility: Disclose all relevant information, disclose delays or deficiencies
Step 1: Discuss with immediate supervisor (unless supervisor is involved)
Step 2: Escalate to next level management / CFO
Step 3: Contact IMA Ethics Hotline (anonymous) for confidential guidance
Step 4: Consult attorney regarding legal obligations
Last resort: RESIGN if the issue cannot be resolved ethically — do NOT prepare or sign documents known to be misleading
Code of conduct: Written statement of company values and expected behaviors
Ethics training: Regular training for all employees
Whistleblower hotlines: Anonymous reporting channels for suspected violations
Ethics officer/Chief Compliance Officer: Oversees the program
Monitoring and enforcement: Regular audits, disciplinary procedures

5-9.v: The FCPA prohibits US companies and individuals from BRIBING foreign government officials to obtain or retain business. It also requires accurate books/records and adequate internal controls (the accounting provisions apply to all public companies, even those not engaged in bribery).

Exam Tips: IMA Statement of Ethical Professional Practice — Four Principles: Honesty, Fairness, Objectivity, Responsibility. Four Standards: Competence, Confidentiality, Integrity, Credibility. Conflict resolution: go up the chain, then IMA hotline, then resign — NEVER falsify documents. FCPA = anti-bribery law for US companies dealing with FOREIGN officials. Confidentiality standard: keep info secret UNLESS legally required to disclose. Integrity = avoiding conflicts of interest.

5-10.v: Monitoring ensures that internal controls CONTINUE to operate effectively over time. Controls can deteriorate due to personnel changes, process changes, or new risks. Monitoring catches these deteriorations before they lead to significant failures.

r: Built into ROUTINE operations — continuous feedback
Supervisory review, automated system alerts, exception reports, daily reconciliations
Internal audit reviews, self-assessments, external audits, SOX 404 assessments
Budget variance reviews, operational metrics, KPI dashboards
Design or operation of control does not allow detection/prevention of misstatements
Less severe than material weakness but still important — warrants attention from those charged with governance
Reported to audit committee and external auditor
SIGNIFICANT deficiency (or combination) that results in REASONABLE POSSIBILITY of material misstatement in FS
Must be reported publicly in annual report; external auditor must report

v: Tests of DESIGN: Would the control, if operating as designed, prevent or detect a material misstatement?
Tests of OPERATING EFFECTIVENESS: Is the control actually operating as designed, consistently and by the right people?
Walkthroughs: Tracing a transaction from beginning to end — both design and operating effectiveness
Number of control failures reported per period
Percentage of reconciliations completed on time
Audit findings — number and severity
Days to remediate control deficiencies
Percentage of employees completing ethics training
Identify root cause (not just symptom)
Design and implement improved control
Test the new control
Monitor to confirm remediation is effective
Document for SOX/audit purposes

5-10.v: Exam Tips: COSO Monitoring = 5th component — ensures controls CONTINUE to work. Ongoing monitoring = built into daily operations. Separate evaluations = periodic independent reviews. Three levels: Deficiency < Significant Deficiency < Material Weakness. MATERIAL WEAKNESS = must be disclosed publicly. Tests of design vs. tests of operating effectiveness. Walkthrough = trace one transaction end-to-end. Remediation requires ROOT CAUSE analysis.

5-11.v: Business Continuity Planning (BCP) ensures an organisation can continue CRITICAL OPERATIONS during and after a disaster or disruption. It is a key INTERNAL CONTROL over operational risk. Without BCP, even a short disruption can cause catastrophic financial and reputational damage.

r: ENTIRE organisation — all critical business functions
Focused primarily on IT systems and data recovery
Keep the business RUNNING during and after a disruption
RESTORE IT systems and data after a disaster
Operations, supply chain, HR, customer service, communications
BCP is broader — DRP is a SUBSET of BCP

5-11.v: The foundation of BCP. A BIA identifies CRITICAL BUSINESS FUNCTIONS and quantifies the financial and operational impact of disruptions over time.

v: Identify critical processes: which functions are essential to operations and revenue?
Determine Maximum Tolerable Downtime (MTD): how long can each function be interrupted before causing irreversible damage?
Prioritise recovery: functions with lower MTD must be restored first
Quantify impact: financial losses, regulatory penalties, reputational damage per hour/day of downtime

r: Maximum ACCEPTABLE TIME to restore a system or process after a failure
If RTO = 4 hours, systems must be back online within 4 hours
Maximum acceptable data LOSS measured in time — how far back can you roll back?
If RPO = 1 hour, backups must exist at least every hour
Absolute maximum time a business function can be disrupted before catastrophic damage
MTD ≥ RTO (RTO must be within MTD)
Average actual time taken to restore a system after a failure
Fully equipped, mirrored backup facility — systems running in real time
Partially equipped facility — hardware ready, software/data must be loaded
Empty facility with power/connectivity — all equipment must be brought in
Data and applications hosted in cloud — accessible from anywhere

v: Tabletop Exercise: Discussion-based walkthrough of scenarios — no actual systems involved. Cheapest and most common
Simulation Test: Team acts out a disaster scenario and follows the BCP procedures
Parallel Test: Backup systems activated alongside live systems to verify they work without disrupting operations
Full Interruption Test: Actual systems shut down and BCP fully executed — highest risk, most realistic
Natural disasters: floods, earthquakes, hurricanes, fires
Technology failures: system crashes, ransomware, power outages, network failures
Human threats: cyberattacks, employee sabotage, terrorism
Pandemic/epidemic: mass workforce unavailability
Supply chain disruption: key vendor failure

5-11.v: Exam Tips: BCP = entire business continuity. DRP = IT subset of BCP. RTO = how quickly systems must be back (time to recover). RPO = how much data loss is acceptable (time since last backup). Hot site = fastest, most expensive. Cold site = slowest, cheapest. BIA identifies critical functions and MTD. Always test the BCP — untested plans often fail. MTD ≥ RTO always.
